items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.title: Understanding Viral Genome Types and Their Implications for Translation
items.content: Viral genomes can be single- or double-stranded RNA or DNA, with single-stranded genomes designated as positive (+) if they encode viral proteins directly, or negative (-) if they require transcription into complementary RNA. Positive-strand RNA viruses can immediately translate their genome upon entry into the host cell, whereas negative-strand viruses carry transcriptases to transcribe their genome into mRNA.
keyPoints: Single-stranded genomes are designated as positive (+) if they encode viral proteins directly.
Negative-strand (-) genomes require transcription into complementary RNA for translation.
Negative-strand viruses carry transcriptases to facilitate the transcription of their genome upon entry into the host cell.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.title: Capsid Structure and Symmetry in Viruses
items.content: Viral capsids are composed of repeating protein subunits due to limited genomic coding capacity, organized into symmetrical structures. The two primary types of symmetry are icosahedral (spherical) and helical. Icosahedral symmetry involves a structure with 20 triangular faces and multiple axes of symmetry.
keyPoints: Capsids are made from repeating protein subunits due to limited coding capacity.
Icosahedral symmetry is based on an icosahedron with two-, three-, and fivefold axes of symmetry, formed from 20 triangular faces.
Helical symmetry is another common capsid organization.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.title: Distinguishing Viruses from Other Intracellular Pathogens
items.content: Viruses are distinct from other intracellular parasites like viroids, virusoids, prions, and intracellular bacteria. Viroids lack a protein coat and infect plants with circular RNA genomes, while virusoids require viruses for replication. Prions consist of misfolded proteins that propagate by inducing misfolding in normal proteins.
keyPoints: Viruses differ from viroids, which are small, circular, single-stranded RNA pathogens without a protein coat.
Virusoids depend on helper viruses for replication and protein coats.
Prions are infectious misfolded proteins that cause other proteins to misfold.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES
items.title: Why are enveloped viruses highly transmissible?
items.content: Enveloped viruses, such as measles virus, have a lipid membrane that fuses easily with the host cell's plasma membrane or internal membranes. This facilitates efficient delivery of the nucleocapsid to the cytoplasm, enhancing their transmissibility.
keyPoints: Enveloped viruses efficiently infect cells by fusing their viral lipid membrane with the host cell's plasma membrane.
These viruses are highly transmissible due to this fusion mechanism.
The lipid envelope can be disrupted by detergents or organic solvents, allowing inactivation by soap and water or alcohol-based hand sanitizers.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES

items.subtopic: DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES
items.title: How are unenveloped viruses resistant to detergents?
items.content: Unenveloped viruses have a tough protein shell that resists disruption by small-intestine bile salts, allowing them to infect the intestine. They are not inactivated by detergents or organic solvents and require peroxide, hypochlorite, or thorough washing with soap and water for inactivation.
keyPoints: Unenveloped viruses possess a tough protein shell resistant to small-intestine bile salts.
This resistance allows them to infect the intestine.
They are not inactivated by detergents or organic solvents; require peroxide, hypochlorite, or thorough washing for inactivation.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY
items.title: Mechanisms of Viral Entry into Host Cells
items.content: Viruses must bind to specific receptors on the host cell surface and enter through one of three pathways: fusion with the plasma membrane, endocytosis followed by fusion with the endosome, or lysis/pore formation in the endosome. This entry is crucial for subsequent viral replication.
keyPoints: Viruses bind to charged molecules on the cell surface before binding specifically to proteins or carbohydrates.
Entry pathways include: (1) fusion of the envelope with the plasma membrane; (2) endocytosis followed by fusion with the endosome membrane; (3) lysis or pore formation in the endosome.
Endocytosis mechanisms include clathrin-mediated, macropinocytosis, micropinocytosis, and caveolar endocytosis.
Acidification of endocytic vesicles triggers conformational changes for fusion and release of nucleocapsid.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY
items.title: Importance of Endosomal Acidification in Viral Entry
items.content: After viruses enter endocytic vesicles, acidification is critical for conformational changes in viral glycoproteins. This process facilitates the fusion of the viral envelope with the endosome membrane and subsequent release of the nucleocapsid into the cytoplasm.
keyPoints: Endosomal acidification leads to conformational changes in viral glycoproteins.
These changes are necessary for the fusion of the viral envelope with the endosomal membrane.
Release of the nucleocapsid into the cytoplasm is essential for transcription, translation, and replication.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.title: Positive-Strand RNA Viruses: Direct Translation and Polyprotein Processing
items.content: Positive-strand RNA viruses, such as picornaviruses and flaviviruses, have genomes that can be directly translated in the cytoplasm after uncoating. Their genomic RNA is initially translated into a polyprotein, which is subsequently cleaved by viral and cellular proteases to produce nonstructural proteins for genome replication and structural proteins for capsid assembly.
keyPoints: Positive-strand RNA viruses include picornaviruses, caliciviruses, hepeviruses, togaviruses, flaviviruses, and coronaviruses.
Genomic RNA is directly translated into polyproteins in the cytoplasm after uncoating.
Polyproteins are cleaved to generate nonstructural proteins for replication and structural proteins for capsid assembly.
Replication complexes associated with cytoplasmic membranes facilitate viral RNA synthesis.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.title: Negative-Strand RNA Viruses: Transcription and Replication
items.content: Negative-strand RNA viruses, such as rhabdoviruses and paramyxoviruses, possess a genome that is transcribed by virion-associated RNA-dependent RNA polymerase to produce mRNAs. These mRNAs encode replicase and structural proteins. The replicase synthesizes positive-strand RNA from the negative strand, which serves as a template for new negative-strand synthesis.
keyPoints: Negative-strand RNA viruses include rhabdoviruses, filoviruses, paramyxoviruses, influenza viruses, peribunyaviruses, and arenaviruses.
Genome transcription is performed by virion-associated RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.
Replicase synthesizes positive-strand RNA from the negative strand, which then serves as a template for new negative-strand synthesis.
Influenza viruses transcribe in the nucleus using nascent cellular transcripts as primers.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.title: Double-Stranded RNA Viruses: Cytoplasmic Replication
items.content: Double-stranded RNA viruses, such as reoviruses and rotaviruses, have genomes consisting of multiple dsRNA molecules. These are transcribed by virion-associated RNA-dependent RNA polymerases to produce mRNAs encoding both nonstructural and structural proteins. Viral replication and assembly occur in cytoplasmic viral factories.
keyPoints: Double-stranded RNA viruses include reoviruses and rotaviruses.
Genomes consist of multiple dsRNA molecules transcribed by virion-associated polymerases.
Replication and assembly occur in cytoplasmic viral factories.
Progeny viruses are released upon cell lysis.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)
items.title: Herpesvirus Immediate-Early Gene Transcription Mechanism
items.content: Upon entering the host cell, herpesviruses transport their dsDNA genomes to the nucleus. Despite initial histone-mediated silencing, viral enhancers and virion proteins facilitate immediate-early gene transcription by reorganizing chromatin.
keyPoints: Herpesvirus dsDNA is not coated with histones in the virion.
Viral enhancer and virion protein drive chromatin reorganization for immediate-earliy gene expression.
Immediate-early proteins promote early gene transcription, essential for viral DNA synthesis.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)
items.title: Poxvirus Cytoplasmic Replication Strategy
items.content: Unlike most dsDNA viruses, poxviruses replicate in the cytoplasm. They encode necessary enzymes and factors for transcription and genome replication independently of host nuclear machinery.
keyPoints: Poxviruses replicate entirely in the cytoplasm.
Encode their own DNA-dependent RNA polymerase for viral genome transcription.
Early mRNAs produce additional transcription and DNA replication factors.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS
items.title: How Viruses Manipulate Host Cell Machinery for Replication and Growth
items.content: Viruses often inhibit host cellular processes like transcription and protein synthesis to favor their own replication. This can lead to cell injury, necrosis, or apoptosis. Some viruses induce host cell growth by manipulating the cell cycle, such as activating the S phase, which is necessary for viral DNA replication. These actions may involve inactivation of tumor-suppressor genes like p53 and retinoblastoma pRB, potentially leading to oncogenic transformation.
keyPoints: Viruses inhibit host transcription and protein synthesis to optimize their own replication.
Cellular changes include CPE: detachment, rounding up, lysis, or apoptosis.
Papovaviruses, papillomaviruses, adenoviruses induce S phase for viral DNA replication.
Viral mechanisms can inactivate tumor-suppressor genes like p53 and retinoblastoma pRB.
Some viruses may lead to oncogenic transformation and cancer cell immortalization.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS

items.subtopic: VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS
items.title: Host Antiviral Responses and Viral Evasion Mechanisms
items.content: Host cells have intrinsic resistance mechanisms, such as the rhesus macaque Trim5α protein that inhibits HIV-1. However, viruses like HSV can counteract these defenses by degrading antiviral proteins (e.g., PML) through viral gene products like ICP0, an E3 ubiquitin ligase. Despite this, interferon signaling increases the expression of antiviral proteins such as PML and IFI16, which can reduce wild-type HSV infection.
keyPoints: Host cells have intrinsic resistance mechanisms against viruses (e.g., Trim5α inhibits HIV-1).
HSV evades host defenses by degrading PML protein using ICP0.
Interferon signaling increases expression of antiviral proteins like PML and IFI16.
Elevated levels of interferon-stimulated genes can reduce wild-type HSV infection.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS

items.subtopic: ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS
items.title: Understanding Viral Infection Outcomes in Cells
items.content: The outcome of a viral infection within a cell is determined by the balance between proviral and antiviral factors, which dictates whether the cell becomes permissive or nonpermissive for viral replication. Productive infections result in progeny virus production. Persistent infections occur when an infected cell does not die but harbors the virus. Chronic infections involve continuous production of infectious virus. Abortive infections are incomplete; they may lead to cell death due to cytopathic effects, oncogenic transformation, or latent infection where no infectious virus is present but can reactivate later.
keyPoints: Productive infection: progeny virus produced
Persistent infection: virus established without cell death
Chronic infection: continuous production of infectious virus
Abortive infection outcomes: (1) cell death from cytopathic effects, (2) oncogenic transformation, (3) latent infection with potential reactivation
Examples: Merkel cell polyomavirus causes abortive oncogenic infection; hepatitis B virus leads to chronic liver infection; HSV establishes latent neuronal infection
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS
items.title: Clinical Implications of Viral Infection Types
items.content: Different types of viral infections have distinct clinical implications. Productive and chronic infections can lead to ongoing disease states due to continuous virus production. Persistent infections may result in long-term health issues without immediate cell death. Abortive infections, while not producing infectious virus, can still cause significant pathology through cell death or transformation into cancerous cells. Latent infections pose a risk of reactivation, leading to recurrent disease episodes.
keyPoints: Productive and chronic infections: potential for ongoing disease
Persistent infection: long-term health issues without immediate cell death
Abortive infection risks: cell death or oncogenic transformation
Latent infections: risk of reactivation and recurrent disease
Clinical examples: Merkel cell polyomavirus, hepatitis B virus, HSV
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.title: Entry Sites and Mechanisms for Viral Infection
items.content: Viruses must enter the host at specific sites such as mucosal surfaces, body openings, or breaks in the skin. Keratinized skin cells are not viable hosts for viral replication.
keyPoints: Keratinized skin cells are not good host cells for viral replication.
Entry occurs at mucosal surfaces (e.g., oral, respiratory, nasal), through body openings (inhalation or ingestion), or breaks in the skin (mosquito bites).
Examples: Papillomaviruses and HSV enter via skin breaks; Zika and dengue via insect bites.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.title: Primary Replication, Disease, and Incubation Period
items.content: Viruses replicate at the site of entry, causing disease at that site or spreading to cause systemic illness. The incubation period can be as short as 1-2 days if disease manifests at the entry site.
keyPoints: Primary replication occurs at the site of entry with potential for local and systemic spread.
Examples: Influenza in respiratory mucosa, Noroviruses in gastrointestinal tract epithelial cells.
Incubation period can be 1-2 days if disease is at the entry site.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.title: Viral Spread and Pathways
items.content: Viruses may spread through lymphatic or bloodstream routes, or via neuronal pathways. This can lead to systemic disease or encephalitis.
keyPoints: Spread occurs through lymph and bloodstream (viremia) or neuronal pathways.
Examples: Measles virus causes systemic disease with a 10-14 day incubation period; Rabies spreads transsynaptically to cause encephalitis.
HSV-1 can establish latent infection in neurons, potentially causing recurrent infections or encephalitis.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.title: Adaptive Immunity in Viral Infections: Mechanisms and Effects
items.content: The adaptive immune response targets viral infections by activating T cells and B cells. CD4+ and CD8+ T cells recognize viral peptides presented on infected cells, leading to cytokine production or cell-killing mechanisms. B cells produce antibodies that neutralize virions or promote their destruction through phagocytosis, antibody-dependent cytotoxicity, and complement-mediated lysis.
keyPoints: Adaptive immunity involves CD4+ and CD8+ T cells recognizing viral peptides on infected cells.
Antibodies produced by B cells can neutralize virions or promote their destruction.
The adaptive immune response targets both virions and infected cells to clear infection.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.title: Long-Term Effects of Viral Infections: Understanding PAIS
items.content: Persistent infections can lead to long-term effects due to ongoing immune responses or acute infection sequelae. Conditions like long COVID and post-acute infection syndromes (PAIS) highlight the chronic symptoms that may arise from persisting viral replication, autoimmune activation, altered microbiota, or tissue damage.
keyPoints: Long-term effects can result from persistent infections or acute infection sequelae.
Conditions such as long COVID and PAIS illustrate these chronic symptoms.
Potential causes include persisting viral replication, autoimmune responses, altered microbiota, or tissue damage.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.title: Viral Evolution: Mechanisms and Implications for Pandemics
items.content: Viral evolution occurs through mutations, reassortment, and recombination. RNA viruses often mutate due to error-prone replication, leading to new variants under selective pressures. Influenza viruses can undergo antigenic shift via genome reassortment, while both DNA and RNA viruses can experience recombination. These evolutionary processes enable viruses to infect new host species or jump species barriers, as seen with SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2.
keyPoints: RNA viruses mutate frequently due to error-prone replication.
Influenza undergoes antigenic shift through genome reassortment.
Recombination can occur in both DNA and RNA viruses, leading to new viral variants.
Evolutionary changes enable viruses to infect new host species or jump species barriers.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.title: Genotyping and Tracking of Viral Strains
items.content: Molecular techniques such as direct sequencing, analysis of polymorphisms in restriction endonuclease cleavage sites, and PCR are essential for identifying genotypic markers in virus isolates. These methods allow precise definition of viral strains, crucial for understanding the epidemiology of viruses like influenza and measles which typically have one dominant strain at a time. In contrast, viruses such as HIV or HSV exhibit multiple co-circulating strains due to their high genetic variability.
keyPoints: Direct sequencing provides the most precise definition of a viral strain.
Influenza and measles usually show one prevalent strain in the population at any given time.
HIV and HSV have many strains circulating simultaneously, each identifiable by molecular techniques.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.title: Detection and Quantification of Infectious Viruses
items.content: Biologic assays are necessary for detecting infectious viruses, measuring their ability to infect cells or cause cytopathic effects (CPE). For instance, SARS-CoV-1 was first isolated using Vero cell cultures where the presence of CPE confirmed infection. These assays help in both clinical diagnostics and scientific research by quantifying viral load and assessing replication.
keyPoints: Biologic assays measure infectivity through animal disease induction or cultured cell infection causing CPE.
SARS-CoV-1 was isolated using Vero cell cultures with detection of CPE.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.title: Visualization of Viral Particles with Electron Microscopy
items.content: Electron microscopy (EM) is essential for visualizing viral particles directly due to their size being below the resolution limit of light microscopes. EM can be used in negative staining or transmission modes, and it has been instrumental in detecting viruses like SARS in cell cultures.
keyPoints: Viruses smaller than light microscope resolution require EM for visualization.
Negative staining and transmission EM are methods to visualize virions.
EM was crucial for first visualizing SARS viral particles.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.title: Detection of Viral Nucleic Acids Using PCR and RT-PCR
items.content: Viral nucleic acids are detected using amplification methods such as PCR with specific primers or reverse transcription followed by PCR for RNA viruses. These techniques allow for the detection and quantification of viral DNA and RNA in clinical samples, including high-throughput measurements like HIV load.
keyPoints: PCR and RT-PCR amplify small numbers of viral nucleic acids.
High-throughput instruments measure HIV load using these methods.
HSV-1 DNA can be rapidly measured in cerebrospinal fluid for diagnosing HSV encephalitis.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.title: Quantification of Viruses Using TCID50 and PFU Assays
items.content: Viral infectivity can be quantified using endpoint dilution assays to determine the tissue culture infectious dose for 50% of cultures (TCID50) or by plaque assays that measure plaque-forming units (PFUs). These methods help in understanding the ratio of viral particles to infectious units, which is typically much greater than 1.
keyPoints: Viral infectivity quantified by TCID50 and PFU assays.
TCID50 measures dilution where half cultures are infected.
PFU titer defined by plaques formed per volume of virus stock.
Particle-to-PFU ratio is usually 10–1000, indicating many non-infectious particles.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a component of the minimal virion particle?
items.explanation: The minimal virion particle consists of nucleic acids and a protein shell, but not ribosomes.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.question: What is the primary function of the viral capsid?
options: To provide energy for the virus
items.explanation: The capsid protects the viral genome within it.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.question: How are negative-strand RNA genomes translated into proteins?
options: They can be directly translated upon entry
They must first be copied into complementary RNA molecules
They require host cell DNA polymerase
They utilize viral ribosomes for translation
items.explanation: Negative-strand RNA genomes must be transcribed into complementary RNA before they can be translated.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.question: Which symmetry is NOT typically used in the construction of viral capsids?
items.explanation: Viral capsids are usually organized with icosahedral or helical symmetry, not tetrahedral.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES
items.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes a characteristic feature of enveloped viruses?
options: They are resistant to disruption by detergents or organic solvents.
Their lipid envelope allows them to fuse easily with host cell membranes, facilitating infection.
They have a tough protein shell that resists bile salts in the small intestine.
They cannot be inactivated by soap and water or alcohol-based hand sanitizers.
items.explanation: Enveloped viruses fuse easily with host cell membranes due to their lipid envelope, aiding infection.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES

items.subtopic: DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES
items.question: What is a key difference between enveloped and unenveloped viruses regarding inactivation?
options: Unenveloped viruses are easily disrupted by detergents or organic solvents.
Enveloped viruses can be inactivated by soap and water or alcohol-based hand sanitizers.
Both types of viruses require peroxide or hypochlorite for inactivation.
Enveloped viruses have a tough protein shell that resists bile salts.
items.explanation: Enveloped viruses can be disrupted by detergents and alcohol-based sanitizers, unlike unenveloped viruses.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES

items.subtopic: DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion used in the classification of viruses?
options: Type of genomic nucleic acid (RNA or DNA)
Presence or absence of an envelope
items.explanation: Virus classification is based on criteria like nucleic acid type and capsid symmetry, not size.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a mechanism by which viruses enter host cells?
options: Fusion with the surface plasma membrane
Endocytosis followed by fusion with the endosome membrane
Lysis of the endosome or formation of pores in the endosome
Direct penetration through the nuclear envelope
items.explanation: Viruses do not directly penetrate through the nuclear envelope as a method of entry; they enter via fusion, endocytosis, or lysis.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY
items.question: Which process is triggered by viral binding to specific molecules on the cell surface?
options: Endocytosis or fusion with the cellular plasma membrane
items.explanation: Viral binding to specific molecules on the cell surface triggers endocytosis or fusion with the cellular plasma membrane.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY
items.question: What leads to conformational changes in viral glycoproteins after entry into endocytic vesicles?
items.explanation: Acidification of endocytic vesicles leads to conformational changes in viral glycoproteins.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: Which type of RNA virus has its genome directly translated into a polyprotein in the cytoplasm after uncoating?
items.explanation: Positive-strand RNA viruses have genomes that are directly translated into polyproteins in the cytoplasm after uncoating.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: Where does replication of influenza virus take place?
items.explanation: Influenza viral transcription and genome replication occur in the nucleus.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: Which type of virus uses both negative- and positive-sense coding for its RNA genomes?
items.explanation: Some bunyaviruses and arenaviruses use ambisense coding, utilizing both negative- and positive-sense RNA.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.question: How do double-stranded DNA viruses of the papovavirus family avoid epigenetic silencing by host cells?
options: By being coated with nucleosomal chromatin
By hiding their genomes with a viral protein
By using host cell RNA polymerase I
items.explanation: Papovavirus and papillomavirus genomes are coated with nucleosomal chromatin, avoiding recognition as foreign by the host.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is essential for immediate-early gene transcription in herpesviruses?
options: A viral enhancer and a virion protein that uses host enzymes to drive chromatin reorganization
Host cell histones without any modifications
items.explanation: A viral enhancer and a virion protein that uses host enzymes to drive chromatin reorganization allow immediate-early gene transcription in herpesviruses.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)
items.question: Where do poxviruses replicate their dsDNA genome?
items.explanation: Poxviruses replicate entirely in the cytoplasm, which is unusual for a dsDNA virus.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)
items.question: What process do parvoviruses use to deal with their ssDNA genome upon entering an infected cell?
options: The ssDNA is directly translated into proteins
Host enzymes copy the ssDNA into dsDNA, which is then transcribed
The ssDNA integrates into the host's chromosomes
Reverse transcription of ssRNA to dsDNA
items.explanation: Parvoviruses use host cell enzymes to copy their ssDNA genome into dsDNA, which is then transcribed by RNA polymerase II.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS
items.question: Which of the following best describes how some viruses optimize their replication within host cells?
options: By promoting apoptosis to eliminate infected cells.
By inducing cellular macromolecular processes such as transcription and protein synthesis.
By inhibiting cellular macromolecular processes like transcription and protein synthesis.
By enhancing the immune response of the host cell.
items.explanation: Viruses inhibit host cell transcription and protein synthesis to optimize their replication by using the host's machinery.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS

items.subtopic: VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS
items.question: What is the role of Epstein-Barr virus in infected B cells?
options: It induces apoptosis to limit viral spread.
It promotes host cell lysis for immediate release of new virions.
It induces proliferation to amplify its latent-infection host cell.
It inhibits cellular growth to conserve resources.
items.explanation: Epstein-Barr virus induces B cell proliferation to enhance its latent infection.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS

items.subtopic: VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS
items.question: Which of the following best describes the function of the rhesus macaque Trim5α protein?
options: It promotes viral replication by enhancing host cell transcription.
It inhibits HIV type 1 infection soon after the viral core enters the cytoplasm.
It degrades cellular tumor-suppressor genes to facilitate viral DNA replication.
It induces programmed cell death in infected cells.
items.explanation: Trim5α protein inhibits HIV type 1 infection soon after the viral core enters the cytoplasm as a host resistance factor.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS

items.subtopic: ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS
items.question: Which type of viral infection is characterized by the continual production of infectious virus?
items.explanation: A chronic infection results in the continuous production of infectious virus.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS
items.question: What is a latent viral infection?
options: An infection where no infectious virus is produced, but it can reactivate later.
An infection that results in immediate cell death.
A type of infection where the virus continually produces progeny.
An infection that immediately transforms cells into cancerous ones.
items.explanation: In a latent infection, no infectious virus is found but it can reactivate at a later time.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS
items.question: What can occur in an abortive viral infection?
options: The cell may die due to cytopathic effects.
The cell becomes permissive for viral replication.
The virus establishes a chronic infection.
items.explanation: In abortive infections, the cell may die if enough cytopathic effects are exerted.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.question: Which route is NOT typically used by viruses to enter a host?
items.explanation: Keratinized skin cells are not viable for viral replication, so viruses do not typically enter through them.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary site of infection for influenza viruses?
items.explanation: Influenza viruses primarily infect the respiratory mucosa.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.question: How does measles virus spread within the body after initial replication?
options: Via direct contact with skin cells
items.explanation: Measles virus spreads from the primary site to secondary sites through the lymph and bloodstream.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.question: Which receptors recognize viral ssRNA?
items.explanation: Viral ssRNA is recognized by Toll-like receptor 7 (TLR7) and TLR8.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.question: Which mechanism allows antibodies to neutralize viral infectivity?
options: By preventing binding of virions to receptors, entry, uncoating, or other steps in infection.
By directly destroying the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.
By inducing apoptosis in infected cells without involving immune cells.
By converting viral antigens into non-infectious particles.
items.explanation: Antibodies can neutralize viral infectivity by preventing binding to receptors, entry, uncoating, or other steps in infection.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.question: What is the primary reason for sequence changes frequently occurring in viral RNA genomes?
options: The presence of error-prone RNA-dependent RNA polymerases without editing functions.
High fidelity of DNA replication machinery in host cells.
Efficient proofreading mechanisms within viral capsids.
Stable integration into the host genome.
items.explanation: Sequence changes occur because viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerases are error-prone and lack editing functions.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.question: What is referred to as antigenic shift in influenza viruses?
options: Small changes due to sequence variation.
Major changes due to genome reassortment and acquisition of new genome segments.
Gradual accumulation of mutations over time.
Recombination between two strains of virus.
items.explanation: Antigenic shift refers to major changes in influenza viruses due to genome reassortment and acquisition of new genome segments.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.question: Which factor can lead to the emergence of more fit viral variants during a pandemic?
options: Immune pressure, drug selection, or host restriction.
Decreased mutation rates in viral genomes.
items.explanation: More fit viral variants can emerge under immune pressure, drug selection, or host restriction.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.question: Which molecular technique provides the most precise definition of a viral strain?
options: Analysis of polymorphisms in restriction endonuclease cleavage sites
items.explanation: Direct sequencing is the most precise method for defining a viral strain.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.question: Which of the following viruses typically have only one prevalent strain in the population at a given time?
items.explanation: Influenza virus and measles virus usually have one prevalent strain in the population at a given time.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.question: How was SARS-CoV-1 virus first isolated?
options: By using PCR analysis on blood samples
By introducing an oropharyngeal swab sample into Vero cell cultures
items.explanation: SARS-CoV-1 virus was first isolated by introducing an oropharyngeal swab sample into Vero cell cultures.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.question: Which method can be used to visualize SARS viral particles in sections of Vero cells?
items.explanation: SARS viral particles were first visualized in sections of Vero cells using negative-staining EM.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.question: What is the purpose of using multiple primers in a multiplex PCR reaction?
items.explanation: Multiple primers in a multiplex PCR reaction are used to detect multiple pathogens.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.question: What is the principle behind detecting viral antigens using enzyme immunosorbent assay (EIA)?
options: Viral particles are visualized by negative staining.
Antiviral antibodies are immobilized and react with patient samples.
Virus spreads cell to cell forming plaques.
items.explanation: In EIA, an antiviral antibody is immobilized on a substrate, followed by incubation with the patient's sample and detection using an enzyme-linked antibody.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.question: How can viral infectivity be quantitatively measured in a plaque assay?
options: By measuring the number of virion particles using EM.
By counting the plaques formed on a cellular monolayer.
items.explanation: In a plaque assay, viral infectivity is measured by counting the number of plaques formed on a cellular monolayer.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.statement: Viruses have a limited coding capacity and therefore their capsids are made from repeating protein subunits.
items.explanation: Viral genomes have limited coding capacity, necessitating the use of repeating protein subunits for capsid construction.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.statement: All viruses have a lipid envelope as part of their structure.
items.explanation: Not all viruses have a lipid envelope; some only have a protein capsid.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES
items.statement: The highest level of viral classification relevant to medical virology is the kingdom.
items.explanation: In medical virology, classification into families and genera is more relevant than kingdoms.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES

items.subtopic: DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES
items.statement: Unenveloped viruses are resistant to inactivation by detergents or organic solvents.
items.explanation: Unenveloped viruses have a tough protein shell, making them resistant to such agents.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DNA viruses | CLASSIFICATION OF VIRUSES

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY
items.statement: All viruses enter host cells by fusion with the surface plasma membrane.
items.explanation: Viruses can also enter via endocytosis or lysis of the endosome.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY
items.statement: Endocytosis is a mechanism that viruses use to gain entry into host cells.
items.explanation: Viruses can enter host cells via endocytosis, among other mechanisms.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION IN CELLS | VIRAL ENTRY

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.statement: The replication of positive-strand RNA viruses occurs in association with cytoplasmic membranes.
items.explanation: Positive-strand RNA virus replication takes place in replication complexes associated with cytoplasmic membranes.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.statement: The genome of double-stranded DNA viruses is always transcribed by viral RNA polymerases.
items.explanation: Double-stranded DNA viruses are typically transcribed by host cell RNA polymerase II, not viral RNA polymerases.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)
items.statement: Retroviruses integrate their DNA into the host cell's chromosomes to form a provirus.
items.explanation: The retroviral integrase catalyzes the integration of viral DNA into the host cell's chromosomes, forming a provirus.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)
items.statement: Poxviruses require nuclear enzymes for their replication.
items.explanation: Poxviruses replicate entirely in the cytoplasm and encode many of the necessary enzymes themselves.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS
items.statement: HSV infection leads to an increase in PML and IFI16 protein expression due to interferon signaling.
items.explanation: Interferon signaling increases PML and IFI16 protein levels, which can reduce wild-type HSV infection.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS

items.subtopic: VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS
items.statement: HSV ICP0 gene product promotes the expression of PML protein to enhance antiviral mechanisms.
items.explanation: ICP0 promotes degradation of PML, thwarting its antiviral mechanism.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL EFFECTS ON THE HOST CELL | HOST ANTIVIRAL RESPONSES AND VIRAL ANTAGONISTIC MECHANISMS

items.subtopic: ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS
items.statement: A productive infection results in no progeny virus being produced.
items.explanation: In a productive infection, progeny virus is indeed produced.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS
items.statement: An abortive oncogenic infection can lead to the transformation of cells into cancerous ones.
items.explanation: Abortive infections may result in oncogenic transformation.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ☑ TYPES OF CELLULAR INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.statement: The innate immune response recognizes specific viral antigens.
items.explanation: The innate immune response recognizes general patterns of viral molecules, not specific antigens.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.statement: Rabies virus can spread to the central nervous system via neuronal pathways.
items.explanation: Rabies virus spreads transsynaptically from the periphery to the central nervous system.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.statement: Antibodies promote phagocytosis and complement-mediated lysis by binding to viral antigens on infected cells.
items.explanation: Antibodies can bind to viral antigens on the surface of virions and infected cells, promoting phagocytosis and complement-mediated lysis.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.statement: Viral evolution is solely driven by recombination in RNA viruses.
items.explanation: While recombination can occur, especially under special circumstances, viral evolution also involves mutation and reassortment.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.statement: Genomic sequencing studies of SARS-CoV-2 have identified multiple major strains circulating simultaneously.
items.explanation: SARS-CoV-2 genomic sequencing has identified several major strains at any given time.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.statement: PCR analysis is the most precise method for defining a viral strain.
items.explanation: Direct sequencing, not PCR analysis, provides the most precise definition of a viral strain.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.statement: Hemagglutination can be used to measure the titer of viruses that agglutinate red blood cells.
items.explanation: Viral titers are measured by hemagglutination as the inverse of the last dilution causing agglutination.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.statement: The particle-to-PFU ratio for viruses infecting humans is typically less than 1.
items.explanation: The particle-to-PFU ratio is always much greater than 1, indicating many non-infectious particles.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.reason: They must enter cells to replicate and propagate themselves.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE
items.assertion: Viruses can replicate independently without a host cell.
items.reason: They have all necessary enzymes for replication within their structure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; viruses cannot replicate independently and do not contain all necessary enzymes.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · Principles of Medical Virology | VIRUS STRUCTURE

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Reoviruses replicate their genomes in cytoplasmic viral factories.
items.reason: The replication of reovirus involves the assembly of dsRNA molecules and capsids within these specialized structures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why reoviruses replicate in cytoplasmic viral factories.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Adenovirus genomes are recognized as foreign by the host cell's epigenetic mechanisms.
items.reason: Adenoviruses coat their DNA with a viral protein to prevent recognition as foreign.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because adenovirus genomes are not recognized as foreign due to being coated with a viral protein.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · VIRAL REPLICATION STRATEGIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.assertion: The incubation period for measles reflects multiple rounds of viral replication and spread.
items.reason: The incubation period from exposure to clinical symptoms is 10–14 days.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)
items.assertion: HSV-1 establishes a latent infection in the cell body of sensory neurons.
items.reason: HSV-1 enters the axon of a sensory neuron after primary infection at mucosal surfaces.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 1)

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.assertion: Persistent infections can lead to long-term effects on the host.
items.reason: These effects may result from persisting viral replication or antigens, activation of autoimmune responses, alteration of endogenous bacteria or viruses, and/or irreparable tissue damage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why persistent infections can lead to long-term effects.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION
items.assertion: Antigenic drift refers to major changes in influenza viruses due to genome reassortment.
items.reason: Genome reassortment leads to new genetic compositions and is responsible for antigenic shift, not drift.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because antigenic drift involves small changes due to sequence variation, while the reason correctly describes antigenic shift.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · ■ STAGES OF INFECTION OF A HOST (part 2) | VIRAL EVOLUTION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS
items.assertion: Biologic assays are necessary to detect and measure infectious virus.
items.reason: Infectivity can be measured by the ability to infect cultured cells and cause CPE.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why biologic assays are needed.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · MOLECULAR EPIDEMIOLOGY OF VIRUSES | DETECTION AND QUANTIFICATION OF VIRUSES | DETECTION OF INFECTIOUS VIRUS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.assertion: Viral nucleic acids can be detected using PCR with specific primers.
items.reason: PCR amplifies very small numbers of viral nucleic acid molecules for detection and quantification.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
items.assertion: Immunofluorescence can be used to detect viral antigens in clinical specimens.
items.reason: This method involves fixation and permeabilization of cells followed by reaction with fluorophore-conjugated antibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Principles of Medical Virology · DETECTION OF VIRAL PARTICLES, THEIR COMPONENTS, AND VIRAL GENE PRODUCTS
